Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to fit in studying around my other responsibilities, but with limited resources and everything else going on, I'd often end up cramming in sessions whenever I could grab a free hour or two. I learn best by doing things hands-on, so I'd spend a lot of time just working through practice problems rather than reading. Some weeks I'd manage maybe nine hours total, but it was rarely consistent or planned out properly.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and put in more effort. Being 27 and juggling other responsibilities means I couldn't always stick to a perfect schedule. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied more than any internal motivation did.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or a message from one of my peer support group members that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd remember I hadn't checked in for a while and feel guilty about it. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse or explore—I needed a specific reason to be there. The peer support intervention actually helped because my group would nudge me when things were due.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. It never became automatic for me. The times I logged in most consistently were when my peer support group was active and we were checking in with each other. That external accountability helped more than any habit I developed on my own. Left to my own devices, I'd forget about the LMS for days at a time.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually helpful because connecting with other students made the material feel more real. When I could actually do something practical or work through problems with peers, that stuck with me better than just watching videos. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so anything interactive or hands-on felt more worthwhile. The peer interactions, even though I only had about seven of them, were genuinely useful.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the video content—over two hours of watching feels like a lot, but I often had it playing while doing other things, not really absorbing it. Reading materials I'd skim or skip entirely unless they seemed directly relevant to an assignment. I just didn't have the energy or motivation to engage with everything when resources were tight and my anxiety was already high.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to passing the course or if it involved working with other people. When something felt abstract or disconnected from any practical outcome, I'd lose interest fast. My motivation is pretty low generally, so I needed activities to prove their value quickly or I'd move on to something else.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford to spend time on. With my assignment completion already being low, I focused whatever limited energy I had on the required stuff. Required activities had consequences attached, which gave them urgency. Optional ones just felt like extra pressure I didn't need when I was already anxious about keeping up.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I'd download materials and work through them on paper because that's how I learn best—physically writing things out, making diagrams, working through problems by hand. I also used some external resources and YouTube tutorials when the course materials weren't clicking for me. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems in a notebook or practicing concepts physically that wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. I'd also discuss course material with my peer support group over messaging apps rather than in the LMS forums. My exam score was decent enough that I clearly learned something, but the LMS wouldn't have captured most of that process.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd download a PDF or save a video once, then work from that offline for days or weeks. With my internet access being reliable but my energy being limited, it was easier to have materials ready to go when I had a burst of motivation. The LMS would just show that one download, not the hours I spent with those materials afterward.
Q12: My anxiety would sometimes spike and make it really hard to even think about coursework. Despite having high resilience, there were periods where everything felt overwhelming and I'd just shut down. When competing responsibilities piled up or when I felt like I was falling behind, engaging became almost impossible. The peer support helped pull me back during those times.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or confused, I'd log in but not really do anything productive. Competing responsibilities were a constant issue—being 27 with limited resources means school isn't always the priority, even when it should be. Fatigue especially affected how much I could actually absorb versus just going through the motions.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My anxiety would be too high to concentrate, or I'd be too exhausted to process information. I'd technically be 'studying' but getting nothing from it. Those sessions felt like a waste, but I didn't know how to make them more productive.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is probably why my completion rate was so low even though my actual assignment scores were high when I did submit. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible, even when I knew I could do them well. I'd avoid things until the pressure became unbearable, then rush to complete them at the last minute.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage sometimes. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation I'd been lacking. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and not accomplish much. It wasn't healthy, but it was how I functioned given my low motivation and high anxiety.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though not always positively. Good feedback on my assignment scores—which were actually quite high—gave me temporary confidence. But if feedback was delayed or critical, my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid the course for a while. I needed positive reinforcement to keep going.
Q18: The course structure with 15 online courses was honestly overwhelming. Having so much content made it hard to know where to focus my limited energy. The peer support intervention was the structural element that helped most—having people to check in with gave me external structure I couldn't create myself.
Q19: The sheer volume of content made engagement harder. But having discussion forums and opportunities for peer interaction made it easier because that's where I thrived. If the course had been more hands-on and less video-heavy, I probably would have engaged more consistently. The design assumed motivation I didn't have.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my worst days—logging in, clicking around, maybe starting things but not finishing them. High anxiety can make you present without being productive. You might be searching for something that helps you understand, trying different resources, but never settling into actual work.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My exam score was decent and my assignment scores were high when I submitted, but my LMS activity was low. I did a lot of learning offline, through peer conversations outside the system, and by working through materials in my own way. The LMS doesn't capture that kind of engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in and how often, but they miss the quality of that time entirely. They can't tell if someone is anxiously clicking around versus deeply focused. They definitely miss offline studying, peer conversations on other platforms, and the emotional state behind the activity.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious and working through problems because I want to understand, not just to check a box. For me, engagement often happened in conversations with peers or when I was physically working through something hands-on.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group actually kept me in the course, or how my anxiety affected every interaction I had with the material. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials or the moments when I was genuinely happy to be learning something new. The emotional journey is completely invisible.
